score.label: Final validation score
score.desc: Weighted average of three complementary indicators, mapped to an integer 0-100. 80+ is a pass (Sargent 2013's face-validity threshold).

chiNorm.label: sub-score (normalised)
chiNorm.desc: The chi-square statistic after soft-cap mapping to 0-100. χ² = 0 → 100 points; χ² ≥ 0.5 → 0. Weighted at 40%, the heaviest of the three.

ksNorm.label: sub-score (normalised)
ksNorm.desc: The KS D statistic after soft-cap mapping. D = 0 → 100; D ≥ 0.3 → 0.

klNorm.label: sub-score (normalised)
klNorm.desc: KL divergence (in nats) after soft-cap mapping. KL = 0 → 100; KL ≥ 0.3 nats → 0.

weightChi.label: weight (chi-square)
weightChi.desc: Chi-square carries 40% weight, slightly higher than the other two (each 30%), because χ² goodness-of-fit is exactly the method Hirsch 2025 itself uses — strongest methodological alignment.
weightChi.example: Equal weights (0.33/0.33/0.33) would give 86 instead of 87.

weightOthers.label: weight (KS & KL)
weightOthers.desc: KS and KL carry 30% each. They cover blind spots χ² misses: KS catches shape shifts, KL catches probability mass on wrong categories.

stat.label: Raw statistic
stat.desc: The raw value of χ², KS D, or KL, each computed by its own formula before this soft-cap stage.

cap.label: Soft-cap threshold
cap.desc: The stat value at which the score reaches 0. Deliberately loose to avoid Monte Carlo noise triggering false failures.
cap.example: χ² cap = 0.5; KS cap = 0.3; KL cap = 0.3

maxFn.label: max(0, ·) guard
maxFn.desc: Clamp to prevent the score from going negative when stat exceeds cap.

hundredScale.label: Percent-scale factor
hundredScale.desc: Multiplying by 100 maps the [0, 1] range to a familiar 0-100 score.

chiStat.label: chi-square statistic
chiStat.desc: Measures the total categorical deviation between observed and expected distributions. Pearson (1900)'s original formulation, still the default for categorical goodness-of-fit.

obsO.label: observed proportion in category i
obsO.desc: The simulation's share for each category. The 9 categories must sum to 1.

expE.label: expected proportion in category i
expE.desc: The empirical target distribution (Hirsch 2025): what each category should be. Also sums to 1.

residualSquared.label: squared residual
residualSquared.desc: The difference squared. Squaring turns both too-high and too-low residuals into penalties (direction doesn't matter).

sigmaSum.label: sum over all categories
sigmaSum.desc: Add up each category's (Oᵢ−Eᵢ)²/Eᵢ. i runs from 1 to k (total number of categories).

kCategories.label: number of categories
kCategories.desc: How many categories are being compared. NekoServe's cat behavior has 9 categories, so k = 9. Degrees of freedom df = k − 1 = 8.

ksD.label: KS statistic
ksD.desc: Maximum vertical distance between two CDFs. Kolmogorov (1933) and Smirnov (1948)'s non-parametric test.
ksD.example: D = 0.079 → largest gap is 7.9 percentage points

fSim.label: simulation CDF
fSim.desc: Running total of the simulation's 9 category shares, producing a step-function that runs from 0 to 1.
fSim.example: F_sim(SOCIALIZING) = sum of OUT_OF_LOUNGE + RESTING + SOCIALIZING shares

fEmp.label: empirical CDF
fEmp.desc: Running total of Hirsch 2025's 9 expected shares, in the same category order for apples-to-apples comparison.

maxOp.label: maximum over x
maxOp.desc: Scan all category points x; pick the one where the two CDFs' distance is largest. That worst-case gap is the global KS distance.
maxOp.example: Across 9 categories, SHELF has the largest gap 0.079 → D = 0.079

absValue.label: absolute value
absValue.desc: Magnitude only, ignore sign. Over and under-sampling in either direction count equally.

dkl.label: Kullback-Leibler divergence
dkl.desc: Information-theoretic distance measuring 'how bad is P as an approximation to Q'. Kullback & Leibler (1951). Larger = more information lost.

pDist.label: simulation distribution
pDist.desc: The distribution under test (NekoServe's simulated 9-category shares). Goes in the first KL argument as 'the approximation being evaluated'.

qDist.label: empirical distribution
qDist.desc: The ground-truth distribution (Hirsch 2025's 9 shares). Goes in the second KL argument as the 'baseline', which sets KL's directionality.

pi.label: simulation probability (category i)
pi.desc: P's probability for one specific category (e.g. simulation RESTING 35.4% → p_RESTING = 0.354). Each pᵢ in [0, 1]; sum = 1.

qi.label: empirical probability (category i)
qi.desc: Q's probability for one specific category (e.g. empirical RESTING 31.7% → q_RESTING = 0.317). Appears in the denominator, so code clamps it with ε = 1e-9.

logRatio.label: log-ratio
logRatio.desc: Natural log of 'how many times pᵢ is bigger than qᵢ'. Positive when pᵢ > qᵢ, negative when pᵢ < qᵢ, zero when equal. This is the source of KL's asymmetry.

phat.label: sample proportion
phat.desc: Successes / total trials (e.g. SHELF = 4209 / 8547 = 0.492). The centre of the Wilson CI estimate.

nSize.label: sample size
nSize.desc: Total number of observations. Larger n → narrower, more precise CI. Hirsch 2025 uses n = 8547 in-lounge scans for vertical levels.

zValue.label: confidence critical value
zValue.desc: Standard normal quantile. 95% CI uses z = 1.96 (2.5% tail each side); 99% CI would use z = 2.576.

biasTerm.label: bias correction
biasTerm.desc: Wilson's extra shrinkage over Wald. Pulls extreme proportions (p̂ near 0 or 1) toward the middle so the CI doesn't collapse.

plusMinus.label: two-sided CI width
plusMinus.desc: Confidence interval extends equally in both directions: lower = centre − margin, upper = centre + margin.

intro: After a simulation run, this page compares the observed cat-behavior distribution against Hirsch et al. (2025)'s 227 hours of direct observation. A composite score of 80 or higher means the scenario closely matches the empirical baseline.
needsRun: No simulation has run yet. Go to Settings and press Run first.
noIssues: No significant deviations; the distribution shape matches the empirical observation.

pass.lead: Composite score {{total}} / 100 meets the Hirsch 2025 nine-state alignment threshold; χ² = {{chi}}, KS D = {{ks}}, KL = {{kl}} nats.
pass.weakest: Among the three sub-metrics, {{dim}} scores lowest ({{score}}), though it is still within an acceptable band.
pass.suggestionFallback: No single category deviates beyond the significance cut-off; the overall shape matches the paper sample.

marginal.lead: Composite score {{total}} / 100: heading the right way, but not yet at the 80-point pass threshold. χ² = {{chi}}, KS D = {{ks}}, KL = {{kl}} nats.
marginal.weakest: Among the three sub-metrics, {{dim}} scores lowest ({{score}}), which is the main drag on the composite.
marginal.suggestionFallback: No single category is dramatically off; the whole distribution is slightly displaced. Consider increasing the effective sample (longer simulation or more cats).

fail.lead: Composite score {{total}} / 100: the shape does not match Hirsch 2025. χ² = {{chi}}, KS D = {{ks}}, KL = {{kl}} nats.
fail.weakest: Among the three sub-metrics, {{dim}} scores lowest ({{score}}), which is the clearest signal of structural mismatch.
fail.suggestionFallback: The deviation is spread across several categories rather than one. Revisit whether the scenario truly matches the paper setup (27 cats, large café, 12 h with warm-up).

fitNoteBody: The simulator's per-state time share is compared against Hirsch et al. (2025): 227 hours of direct observation of 27 cats at a Stockholm cat café. Three complementary statistics align the two distributions, so every time you tweak Settings and re-run, this score moves accordingly.
fitNoteChi: Chi-square fit: sensitive to per-category magnitude, catches a single state being far off.
fitNoteKs: KS cumulative gap: tracks overall shape via the largest cumulative-distribution gap.
fitNoteKl: KL divergence: information-theoretic distance from the empirical baseline, penalising probability mass placed in the wrong category.

abandonRate.tooHigh: Customer abandon rate {{observed}}% is above Hirsch's expected [{{min}}%, {{max}}%] range; the cafe may be over-crowded or short on seats/staff.
abandonRate.tooLow: Customer abandon rate {{observed}}% is below Hirsch's expected [{{min}}%, {{max}}%] range; demand may be too sparse to surface realistic stress.

noCatVisitRate.tooHigh: {{observed}}% of customers leave with no cat visit, above Hirsch's expected [{{min}}%, {{max}}%]; cat supply may be too low or interaction time too short.
noCatVisitRate.tooLow: Only {{observed}}% of customers leave with no cat visit, below Hirsch's expected [{{min}}%, {{max}}%]; the simulator generates more interaction than the paper observed (high cat supply / long interaction time).

smallSampleTitle: Sample size is small; scores will swing widely
smallSampleBody: This run only has {{catMinutes}} cat-minutes of observation, far below Hirsch 2025's 6,129 cat-minutes. Re-running with a different seed could shift the score by 30+ points. For a stable score, load the "Paper sample (Hirsch 2025)" preset on the Settings page, or raise catCount and simulationDuration manually.
behaviorCompare: Nine-state behavior distribution (Hirsch Figure 3 vs simulation)

framework.heading: 1. Verification & validation framework
framework.intro: I use the simulation field's standard multi-metric composite validation approach: any single statistic has blind spots, and three complementary indicators cover each other's weaknesses. This isn't something I invented — the V&V literature is explicit about it:
framework.sargent: <cite>Sargent (2013)</cite>: in the 2013 Winter Simulation Conference V&V guidelines, recommends that face-validity scores use a weighted composite of multiple indicators, since single statistics are fooled by outliers or distribution shape shifts. My 0.4·χ² + 0.3·KS + 0.3·KL is a direct implementation of that principle.
framework.kleijnen: <cite>Kleijnen (1995)</cite>: the default for categorical-distribution comparison is χ² goodness-of-fit. My χ² test follows this paper.
framework.rubner: <cite>Rubner et al. (2000)</cite>: KL divergence, EMD, and KS-style max gaps each have different failure modes; using multiple is how you avoid those failure modes compounding.
framework.alignmentBody: Hirsch 2025 itself uses χ² goodness-of-fit tests to validate their observation data (χ²(2) = 1234.2 for vertical level; see the four tests listed under "benchmark provenance" below). I'm not picking χ² out of thin air — it's the same method the paper uses.
framework.compositeFormulaHint: Final validation score is a weighted average of three sub-metrics: chi-square 40%, KS 30%, KL 30%. Weights follow Sargent 2013's guidance, not arbitrary.
framework.compositeFormulaLong: χ²_norm, KS_norm, KL_norm are the 0-100 soft-capped sub-scores. χ² gets a slightly heavier weight (40% vs 30% vs 30%) because it's the same goodness-of-fit method Hirsch 2025 uses, which gives it the strongest methodological pedigree. Switching to Rubner 2000's EMD would require re-tuning these weights.

complementarity.heading: 2. Why three indicators, not one
complementarity.intro: Each indicator has its own specialty and its own blind spot. Combining three as a composite avoids both false-positive and false-negative judgments:

ks.strength: Where in the CDF the largest deviation sits

kl.strength: Probability mass on wrong category (information loss)

thresholds.heading: 3. How the 80-point cutoff and soft-caps were chosen
thresholds.intro: Each raw statistic is soft-capped into a 0-100 score. Thresholds:
thresholds.chi: The hard χ² critical value at df = 8, α = 0.05 is 15.51 (<cite>Agresti 2013</cite> standard tables). Our soft-cap sits at 0.5, about 30× looser than the hard critical value. Deliberate choice: we're operating in proportion space, not count space, so the critical value needs rescaling; and a loose soft-cap avoids Monte Carlo noise tripping a false-failure.
thresholds.ks: KS critical D at n = 8547 is D₀.₀₅ ≈ 0.0147 (Massey 1951 approximation D₀.₀₅ = 1.36/√n). Our soft-cap 0.3 is about 20× looser — same noise-tolerance rationale.
thresholds.kl: KL has no well-defined critical value. The soft-cap 0.3 nats corresponds to exp(-0.3) ≈ 74% approximation fidelity, near the industry-accepted lower bound.
thresholds.composite: The 80-point pass threshold follows <cite>Sargent (2013)</cite>'s recommended ≥ 0.8 face-validity threshold. Passing means all three angles land in the "acceptable approximation" band.
thresholds.caveat: These soft-cap choices are engineering heuristics, not rigorous hypothesis tests. For strict statistical significance, consult the raw p-values (surfaced below each indicator card), not the 0-100 score.
thresholds.softCapHint: Converts a raw statistic (χ², KS D, KL in nats) to a 0-100 score: stat = 0 gives 100 points; stat ≥ cap gives 0; linear in between.

chi.heading: Chi-square: per-category contribution breakdown
chi.intro: The chi-square statistic is the sum of (O−E)²/E across all 9 categories. The table lists each category's observed (O), expected (E), raw residual (O−E), standardised residual (O−E)/√E, and its contribution to the total chi-square. A large residual in one category pulls the whole chi-square up.
chi.formulaHint: Sum, over every category, of (residual² / expected). Squaring makes both positive and negative residuals count as a penalty; dividing by E prevents large-E categories from dominating by default.
chi.formulaLong: O_i is the observed proportion in category i, E_i the expected (empirical) proportion, k the category count. Degrees of freedom df = k − 1 (minus 1 for the constraint that total probability must sum to 1). NekoServe works in proportion space rather than count space, so critical values need rescaling accordingly.
chi.interpretBody: χ² = {{chi}}, df = {{df}}. Agresti 2013's table gives critical value {{crit}} at α = 0.05; the raw p-value bucket is "{{pBucket}}". In proportion space we also apply a soft-cap (0.5 → 0 points) to avoid Monte Carlo noise triggering a false-failure.

ks.heading: KS cumulative gap: the largest vertical distance between two CDFs
ks.intro: The KS D statistic is the maximum vertical distance between the simulation's cumulative distribution and the empirical one, scanning category-by-category. The red marker on the overlay is that maximum; the table below lists the running cumulative values.
ks.formulaHint: Max vertical distance between two CDFs. F_sim is the simulation cumulative distribution, F_emp the empirical one. D = 0 means the lines overlap perfectly; larger D means there's some x where the gap is biggest.
ks.formulaLong: KS is non-parametric: no distributional assumption, so it handles arbitrary shapes. It's sensitive to global shape shifts but less so to single-small-category differences (cumulation smooths small categories out). The α = 0.05 critical value is approximately 1.36/√n (Massey 1951).
ks.svgAlt: SVG cumulative distribution overlay: empirical vs simulation
ks.interpretBody: D = {{d}}, n = {{n}}, critical D₀.₀₅ = {{crit}} (Massey 1951 approximation 1.36/√n). Raw p-value bucket: "{{pBucket}}". The largest gap sits at {{maxCategory}}. Large n makes strict KS reject easily; we soft-cap deliberately (0.3 → 0 points) to absorb noise. This is a face-validity design choice, not hiding a failure.

kl.heading: KL divergence: information cost of mass on wrong categories
kl.intro: Each category contributes p·log(p/q) nats to the total KL, where p is the simulation share and q is the empirical share. Positive (orange) means the simulation puts too much mass on that category; negative (blue) means too little. The sum is the total KL in nats.
kl.formulaHint: p_i is the simulation probability for category i, q_i the empirical. Each term p·log(p/q) is positive when p > q, negative when p < q. The total is always ≥ 0 (by Jensen's inequality), zero only when the distributions are identical.
kl.formulaLong: KL's intuitive reading: 'when encoding observations using P as an approximation of Q, each observation costs D_KL(P‖Q) extra nats on average.' Asymmetry (D_KL(P‖Q) ≠ D_KL(Q‖P)) means direction matters: NekoServe always puts the empirical Q in the denominator and the simulation P in the numerator, so the penalty targets 'mass where the empirical distribution has none'. A symmetric alternative is Jensen-Shannon divergence or EMD (Rubner 2000).
kl.interpretBody: KL = {{kl}} nats, corresponding to a fidelity of exp(-KL) ≈ {{fidelity}}%. The simulation, treated as an approximation to the empirical distribution, loses about {{loss}}% of information.

provenance.title: Benchmark provenance
provenance.intro: Every proportion in the benchmark carries the paper's original n, a 95% Wilson score confidence interval, and its exact location in the paper for cross-checking. Wilson CI method source:
provenance.wilsonFormulaHint: p̂ is the sample proportion, n the sample size, z = 1.96 for a 95% CI. This expression stays well-behaved even when p̂ is near 0 or 1 or n is small — unlike Wald, which collapses to zero width.
provenance.wilsonFormulaLong: The Wald interval p̂ ± 1.96·√(p̂(1−p̂)/n) is simple but fails when p approaches 0 or 1 (width goes to zero). Wilson (1927) derived this score-test-based correction: add a z²/(2n) bias term, divide by a shrinkage factor (1 + z²/n). Agresti & Coull (1998) showed Wilson keeps actual coverage near the nominal 95% even for small n and extreme proportions, so it's the standard in modern statistics texts.
provenance.paperStatsTitle: Statistical tests used in the paper itself
provenance.paperStatsIntro: Hirsch 2025 runs χ² goodness-of-fit tests on exactly these distributions. Our validation method is aligned with the paper's own analytical approach, not a new invention.

OUT_OF_LOUNGE.zh: 離開顧客區（回到員工/貓咪專屬空間）
OUT_OF_LOUNGE.en: Cat is outside the customer lounge (back-of-house or cat-only zone)

RESTING.zh: 休息、趴臥或睡覺，對外界刺激反應低
RESTING.en: Resting, lying down, or sleeping; low responsiveness

SOCIALIZING.zh: 主動與顧客或其他貓互動、接觸
SOCIALIZING.en: Actively interacting or making contact with people or other cats

HIDDEN.zh: 躲在顧客看不到的隱蔽處（床底、箱內等）
HIDDEN.en: Hidden out of customer sight (under furniture, inside boxes, etc.)

ALERT.zh: 警覺觀察環境，未進入互動或休息
ALERT.en: Alert and scanning the environment, neither interacting nor resting

MOVING.zh: 在空間裡走動、穿梭，沒有特定目的地
MOVING.en: Walking or moving through the space without a clear destination

EXPLORING.zh: 嗅聞、觀察物件或環境細節
EXPLORING.en: Sniffing or investigating objects and surroundings

PLAYING.zh: 追逐、撲抓玩具或同伴的玩耍行為
PLAYING.en: Chasing, pouncing, or playing with toys or other cats

FURNITURE.zh: 位於家具高度（椅子、桌面等中層）

SHELF.zh: 位於貓跳台、層板等高處
SHELF.en: On shelves or cat trees; the elevated level

out_of_lounge.too_high: Cats hide in the cat room too often. Lower per-cat customer load or add more in-lounge perches.
out_of_lounge.too_low: Cats almost never retreat to the cat room. Confirm the cat-only room is available as a private space.

resting.too_high: Too much resting. Try raising arrival rate or lowering catRestProbability.
resting.too_low: Cats barely rest; unrealistic for house cats. Raise catRestProbability or extend catRestDuration.

socializing.too_high: Socializing exceeds Hirsch's 12.8%. Reduce customer-initiated interaction probability or widen personality variance.
socializing.too_low: Not enough socializing; customer experience may suffer. Raise density or extend catInteractionTime.

hidden.too_high: Hiding is excessive: cats are stressed. Reduce customer count or add more perch / cat-room space.
hidden.too_low: Cats barely hide (Hirsch reports ~10.7%); check ALERT and shelf-preference logic.

alert.too_high: Alert share is high; crowding is distressing the cats. Reduce density or speed up exits.
alert.too_low: Alert share is too low; simulation isn't capturing stress signals. Confirm occupancy modifiers apply.

grooming.too_high: Grooming exceeds Hirsch's 4.5%; GROOMING dwell time may be too long.
grooming.too_low: Grooming is suppressed, a stress signal. Try lowering customer density.

moving.too_high: Moving share too high; dwell time may be too short.
moving.too_low: Cats barely move; check activity_level distribution.

exploring.too_high: Exploring exceeds the 0.8% baseline; cats are too restless.
exploring.too_low: Exploring is suppressed, indicating a stressful environment. Lower occupancy or enrich the space.

playing.too_high: Playing exceeds Hirsch's 0.3%; this is rare, check personality vectors.
playing.too_low: Playing is near zero, which matches Hirsch, but a strict zero may mean the PLAYING branch never fires.

floor.too_high: Floor share is high; usually means there aren't enough perches or furniture spots.
floor.too_low: Cats almost never on the floor; you may have overestimated occupancy.

furniture.too_low: Furniture is ignored; review furniture spot placement.

shelf.too_high: Shelf share is high, reflecting stress under crowding (Hirsch 2025 Figure 4b).
shelf.too_low: Shelf share is low; space enrichment may be inadequate.
